item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The letters are just unnerving. Very well done, just as expected from Kaktus.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "There is a special hell for debtors and a hole just your size" is one of those of those lines that will ring in my head forever in a really wonderful way. This is a personal favorite of mine, a classic of economic horror.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Hey, if you see this, can't you link it to the factory hub that now exists? I don't know whether or not this would be an area in which i would be allowed to do so.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the building anxiety and sense of dread through this. I thought it was ambiguous enough how much of the victims going crazy is magic and how much is terror of the repo ghosts. This is perfect as a Factory service - they make distorted versions of useful things. Debt collection is useful to someone.

I have to echo the million other comments above in saying that the ending might have had more punch if it was just something like, the Foundation itself gets a letter like the one pictured, and we're left hanging on the implications of that. Still, this is an awesome concept and really captures the slow-burn panic of being in debt.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits added as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

The fear of debt collectors is very real. Got a chance to speak to djkaktus about this article, and he confirmed that the Factory of Factory Financial Management is in fact, THE Factory. Since there is no Factory hub atm, kaktus recommended a link to the Facotory 001 until a more suitable article comes along. Additionally, the mention of MTF-Alpha-1 could be linked to their spot on the MTF page. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

All instances of SCP-2271-1 refer to a "Factory Financial Management" group, and provides phone numbers and addresses used to contact the company. To date, none of these numbers or addresses have shown any indication of being affiliated with the company in question, or any group whatsoever, but if used to mail a package or make a phone call, will result in some kind of communication with the company. Packages do not require a mail carrier; applying an address label of some kind will result in the parcel disappearing shortly afterwards.

To this:

All instances of SCP-2271-1 refer to a "Factory Financial Management" group, and provides phone numbers and addresses used to contact the company. To date, none of these numbers or addresses have shown any indication of being affiliated with the company in question, or any group whatsoever, but if used to mail a package or make a phone call, will result in some kind of communication with the company. Packages do not require a mail carrier; applying an address label of some kind will result in the parcel disappearing shortly afterwards.

Changing this:

On the third of January, 2014, Overwatch Command received a distress call from Site-91, the previous holding facility of all individuals contained in relation to SCP-2271. After the initial call, Site-91 staff did not respond for an additional eight hours, during which MTF Alpha-1 "Red Right Hand" was mobilized. Due to the remote location of Site-91, MTF A-1 was not able to reach Site-91 before it suddenly detonated its on-site nuclear device.

To this:

On the third of January, 2014, Overwatch Command received a distress call from Site-91, the previous holding facility of all individuals contained in relation to SCP-2271. After the initial call, Site-91 staff did not respond for an additional eight hours, during which MTF Alpha-1 "Red Right Hand" was mobilized. Due to the remote location of Site-91, MTF A-1 was not able to reach Site-91 before it suddenly detonated its on-site nuclear device.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Whether or not this is connected to The Factory (in which case it's not really in their MO), this ultimately feels like it's just over the top debt/debt collection with a "makes you go cuckoo" effect added to it, seemingly played for horror with only hints of humor. While I understand "real" debt horror can be hard to write as something the Foundation would study is hard (never mind that it'd be depressing as hell), this ultimately comes off more as a caricature of the situation than a bona fide horrible account. Obviously no one want to be in a situation such as that described in the article; the problem is that real life debt has many subtleties to it that this one completely lacks: the victims here come off as just manically desperate debt payers, not real people facing debt.

The bureacratic aspects have been handled better by SCP-2210 and the infection effects are the kind of thing that get cheapened by their massive nature: a parent willing to kill their family and ship them off because she/he has no other option to overcome this thing is gut wrenching and impactful; it happening on a regular basis to a town feels like something Bobbles would love to do. To put it comparison, it feels like (you have no idea how much I hate having to reference this) what The Happening is to suicidal behavior.

Sorry, man, but I seriously, seriously don't like this. Perhaps I am taking it more seriously than I should, but if the aim was for satire, it didn't push it far enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No, I don't think it is either. Destruction of humanity? It is Keter…!

Kaktus, is this inspired by recent US events? Financial crisis and such? Or more like a personal event? (but this is indeed a personal question, so I'll understand if you don't reply!)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What gets me about this one?

  • There's no evidence that the target even has student loans to repay.
  • If you're close to someone who gets this, you're doomed, regardless of your credit history.

No one knows why this exists, or what they're actually looking for. It's not repayment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Perhaps indeed a bit over the top, but upvoted anyway.

A letter asking for repayment of an important debt is scary enough, and must resonate - no, make this: obviously resonates with a lot of people. Does it need to be cranked up to 11 that way?

OTOH… I rather like the over-the-top craziness of this. Call me Janus! ;>

And I particular love (in a masochistic way) the second image, with no reference in the text, no context and no explanation whatsoever. Creepy as hell.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A little from both pots, but more from the second, I think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
THERE IS A PLACE IN HELL FOR DEBTORS

So does Factory Financial Management believe that their genuinely owed what they're asking for? Or did FFM turn the must-pay-debts compulsion up to 11 for Site-91, which drove them crazy?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Alternately, if we're trying to keep this in the realm of comparison to reality, you could offer an option for a "deferment." Christ alone knows what the company would ask for to make that happen. I explored a similar brand of anomalous predatory capitalism (I just love that phrase) with SCP-1750.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Suggestion - make the collection agency a tiny bit more reasonable. The amount owed is never decreased, regardless of how insane the victim's payment attempts are? Then what's the point of constantly sending them fresh letters?

Instead, have constant letters acknowledging receipt of the partial payment, and supplying the new total.

TOTAL SUM OWED: $12,563,941,000,000,630,422.75
TOTAL SUM PAID (CURRENCY + ASSETS): $727,173,041.25

AMOUNT REMAINING: $12,563,940,999,999,272,457,381.50

Please pay by the end of the month to avoid being charged interestl!

item.title: too much
item.content:encoded:
i think you overdid it. debt in excess of value of the earth that payments have literally no effect on? indetectible super fast "collectors"? fast enough spread to wipe out a foundation site before the situation could be ascertained? victims basically going insane at the drop of a hat?

it would have been a lot better if it were less absurdly over the top, as it is it doesn't grab me. a more subtle version could have been good. this is bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As if student loans can't get scarier…upvoted. Glad I never had to worry about this issues!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Loan Sharks. Hmm…

Now there's an idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like this threat is a job for MTF Lambda-15, "Ted Olson Galaxy".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm assuming since this isn't tagged with The Factory, it's something different which is good in my opinion. This doesn't seem like the type of the thing The Factory would produce.

Anyways, good article. The last bit cranked up the calamity to 11 a little to quickly for my tastes but it's a minor complaint. Up vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For some reason, I can't help but shake the feeling that maybe this is what contacted Abraham with the whole "first born" thing. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: personally, I don't think the factory is really what you would call a group in the same way that the other GOIs are. It may have human beings that act as representatives for it and human beings who are employed by it, but they are not truly part of the factory. My interpretation is that The Factory does bad stuff because it is a factory and factories do bad stuff, and why is a mystery. It might have some kind of goal, or maybe it just doing what it exists to do and nothing else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
(up there withthe Chaos Insurgency in terms of "we do evil stuff because evil nyahaha")

I didn't depict the CI as evil in my first skip, and don't intend to in the future. I haven't written about The Factory, but if I ever do, they aren't getting the Saturday morning cartoon villain treatment, either. That being said, I'm a shit writer and my depiction of the two likely won't be very influential.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hah. You're right. That's what I was missing.

Will make edits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, congratulations on mining a particularly visceral vein of horror-ore for most of our readership. Hopefully the idea that shadowy repo-entities might buy one's student loan debt is something that continues to haunt the shit of everyone. Ye gods.

That said, I don't care for the fact that the recipients are subject to an extremely long and detailed compulsion effect. I think it would have been much more effective had the behavior of the affected parties been allowed to be naturally documented. I would bet that people would do some pretty crazy shit faced with a debt in the septillions of dollars, collectible by scary ghost monsters, compulsion or no. So instead of the piece resonating because one can imagine the horror of being faced with something like this and being forced into doing horrible things because that's the only option available to you, it's a species of crazy-to-death, where things happen because the narrative needs them to.

I also have my recurring personal thing with GoIs. That's not really affecting my vote on this one, but the addition of the Factory (up there with the Chaos Insurgency1 in terms of "we do evil stuff because evil nyahaha") doesn't really add anything to this, and in fact detracts from the mystery somewhat by providing a pre-packaged narrative.

Footnotes
1. I really hate even typing that name

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very effective. Very scary. Well done.

The end of the world: Loan Sharks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Anomalous student loans. 'Bout time someone made a Keter that was actually scary.

The last addendum doesn't really work, I don't think. It's not lolFoundation, because there's no "lol" to it, but… I guess what it does is ramp up the danger and drama without adding anything to the overall narrative. Novoting for the time being.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed reading this. Is this related to *the* Factory? If so, that is the only thing I didn't care for but I've never been much of a fan of that GOI; just not enough meat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Author, I like the intentional misspellings in the first letter. Did you do that for the sake of earriness? Or am I missing something?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nah, I absolutely get that.

The escalation was intended to show what something like this can do to small populations very quickly, where everybody is scrambling over each other to get their "debt" paid, even at the expense of tearing everybody limb from limb.

The device was activated because one person had the foresight to consider what might happen if an active site suddenly was completely without personnel, or only with personnel desperate to sell off everything they could get their hands on, including skips.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm… I can buy Site-09, and the letter thing makes sense, but it still feels like too quick of scaling in terms of craziness for me to fully buy into it, if that makes sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good questions. To clarify:

The letter they sent over was one that a member of staff at Site-91 had received, which was followed by a site wide outbreak afterwards (during those eight hours). Shit spread fast, yo.

As for the different between the sites, Site-91 was just a high security site, while Site-09 is a solitary confinement site. They're not sure if it'll help, but it's a step.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, but find the final image too silly and the whole incident log too uh… I guess unneeded to be able to vote. Those things just make the skip come to a screeching halt when I was liking it. I guess my main problem is that it's hard to imagine the leap you need to make from "Site collecting 2271 materials" to "Site is in distress and oh yeah it detonated its nukes and sent a letter to the O5s for some reason". Won't shunting everything to Site-09 make the same thing happen again soon anyway, if the logic here is to be believed?

Again it's too much of a jump in the article to make, and I feel like even one incident log in between the discovery and the final incident log would make this a lot more believable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think we are supposed to assume the SCP-2271-2 entities killed them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like how topical this is, I suspect that's why people like it. Overall I found it OK, not that creepy, but still kind of interesting. It is an idea I haven't seen here before so that's good.

I was momentarily confused between the -1 and -A designations, that kind of interrupted my read through, but maybe that was just me.

Also, I found several spelling mistakes in the letter. Was that intentional?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, it's good. The idea is well depicted, and even though there are a few excerpts that made me giggle a bit, it's not going to stop me from (neutrally) upvoting the thing. Also, a nitpick.

Any and all Foundation personnel who become affected by SCP-2271 are to be considered KIA

Why K.I.A? Because they will eventually die? Wouldn't "irrecoverable" or even "M.I.A" work better?

item.title: TRIGGER WARNING WTF
item.content:encoded: Hesitant upvote for the concept and mechanism. I've chatted with you on what I disliked. The last image does nothing for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As a university student, this hit home.

The writing is really good, and the skip is pretty interesting and unique!

Good job!

item.title: When payday is less than your interest-
item.content:encoded:
I haven't written a Keter in a while. Felt like this was a good one to go on.

If you're too young to appreciate it yet, consider yourself fortunate. Debt sucks.

Anyway, my thanks to Djoric for inspiring this, TwistedGears for taking a look at it, and Sally Mae Financial Services for keeping me up at night.

As always, your comments, critiques and criticisms are welcome!

Edit: Image is from https://www.af.mil/News/Photos/igphoto/2000335407/ and is in the public domain.
